facts.text: Hip-hop originated in the Bronx, New York City, in the early 1970s.
DJ Kool Herc is widely credited as one of the founding fathers of hip-hop.
The four traditional elements of hip-hop are DJing, MCing (rapping), breakdancing, and graffiti art.
The Sugarhill Gang's 'Rapper's Delight' (1979) is often considered the first commercially successful hip-hop single.
'Rapper's Delight' was released on September 16, 1979.
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five's 'The Message' (1982) was one of the first hip-hop songs to address social issues.
Run-D.M.C. was the first hip-hop group to appear on the cover of Rolling Stone magazine.
Run-D.M.C.'s collaboration with Aerosmith on 'Walk This Way' (1986) helped bring hip-hop to mainstream rock audiences.
The Beastie Boys' 'Licensed to Ill' (1986) was the first hip-hop album to reach number one on the Billboard 200.
DJ Kool Herc's back-to-school party on August 11, 1973, is considered the birthdate of hip-hop.
Public Enemy's 'It Takes a Nation of Millions to Hold Us Back' (1988) is considered one of the most influential hip-hop albums ever made.
N.W.A's 'Straight Outta Compton' (1988) helped pioneer gangsta rap on the West Coast.
The term 'hip-hop' was popularized by Keith Cowboy of Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five.
Sampling, the practice of reusing portions of existing recordings, has been a foundational production technique in hip-hop since its inception.
The Roland TR-808 drum machine became one of the most iconic instruments in hip-hop production.
Afrika Bambaataa's 'Planet Rock' (1982) introduced electronic music elements to hip-hop using the Roland TR-808.
Tupac Shakur was born on June 16, 1971, in East Harlem, New York City.
The Notorious B.I.G. (Biggie Smalls) was born Christopher George Latore Wallace on May 21, 1972.
The East Coast–West Coast hip-hop rivalry of the 1990s was one of the most intense feuds in music history.
Tupac's album 'All Eyez on Me' (1996) was one of the first double-disc studio albums in hip-hop.
The Notorious B.I.G.'s debut album 'Ready to Die' (1994) is ranked among the greatest hip-hop albums of all time.
Tupac Shakur was fatally shot in Las Vegas on September 13, 1996.
The Notorious B.I.G. was killed in a drive-by shooting in Los Angeles on March 9, 1997.
Dr. Dre's 'The Chronic' (1992) popularized G-funk, a subgenre of hip-hop with heavy synthesizer use.
Snoop Dogg's debut album 'Doggystyle' (1993) debuted at number one on the Billboard 200.
Wu-Tang Clan, formed in Staten Island in 1992, consisted of nine original members.
Wu-Tang Clan's debut album 'Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers)' (1993) is considered a cornerstone of East Coast hip-hop.
Eminem's 'The Slim Shady LP' (1999) won the Grammy for Best Rap Album, launching him to superstardom.
Eminem was born Marshall Bruce Mathers III on October 17, 1972, in St. Joseph, Missouri.
Jay-Z has won 24 Grammy Awards, making him one of the most decorated hip-hop artists of all time.
Jay-Z's 'Reasonable Doubt' (1996) is widely considered one of the greatest debut albums in hip-hop.
Jay-Z was born Shawn Corey Carter on December 4, 1969, in Brooklyn, New York.
Nas's debut album 'Illmatic' (1994) is frequently cited as the greatest hip-hop album of all time.
Lauryn Hill's 'The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill' (1998) won five Grammy Awards, a record for a female artist at the time.
OutKast's 'Speakerboxxx/The Love Below' (2003) won the Grammy for Album of the Year, a first for a hip-hop group.
Kanye West's debut album 'The College Dropout' was released on February 10, 2004.
Kanye West originally gained recognition as a producer for Roc-A-Fella Records before becoming a solo artist.
The term 'bling-bling' was popularized by the Cash Money Records camp in the late 1990s.
Missy Elliott was the first female rapper inducted into the Songwriters Hall of Fame in 2019.
Lil Wayne's 'Tha Carter III' (2008) sold over one million copies in its first week.
Drake holds the record for most Billboard Hot 100 entries of any artist in history.
Drake was born Aubrey Drake Graham on October 24, 1986, in Toronto, Canada.
Kendrick Lamar's 'DAMN.' (2017) won the Pulitzer Prize for Music, a first for a non-classical or non-jazz work.
Kendrick Lamar was born on June 17, 1987, in Compton, California.
The Pulitzer Prize for 'DAMN.' was announced on April 16, 2018.
A Tribe Called Quest is credited with pioneering jazz-rap with their debut album 'People's Instinctive Travels and the Paths of Rhythm' (1990).
The Native Tongues collective included A Tribe Called Quest, De La Soul, and the Jungle Brothers.
De La Soul's '3 Feet High and Rising' (1989) was one of the first hip-hop albums to be inducted into the Library of Congress.
Salt-N-Pepa were the first female rap act to go gold and platinum.
Queen Latifah won a Grammy for Best Solo Rap Performance for 'U.N.I.T.Y.' in 1995.
MC Lyte was the first solo female rapper to release a full-length album with 'Lyte as a Rock' (1988).
The first hip-hop record ever made is often debated, but 'King Tim III (Personality Jock)' by the Fatback Band (1979) predates 'Rapper's Delight' by a few weeks.
Def Jam Recordings was founded by Russell Simmons and Rick Rubin in 1984.
Def Jam's first release was LL Cool J's 'I Need a Beat' in 1984.
LL Cool J's name stands for 'Ladies Love Cool James.'
Grandmaster Flash was a pioneer of turntablism techniques including cutting, scratching, and punch phrasing.
The 'breakbeat,' isolated by DJ Kool Herc, is the rhythmic foundation of hip-hop music.
Afrika Bambaataa founded the Universal Zulu Nation, one of the first hip-hop awareness organizations, in 1973.
The first major hip-hop film was 'Wild Style' (1983), starring real graffiti artists and MCs from New York City.
The documentary 'Style Wars' (1983) chronicled the early graffiti and breakdancing culture of New York.
Breakdancing (breaking) was officially added as an Olympic sport for the 2024 Paris Games.
The term 'MC' originally stood for 'Master of Ceremonies' in the context of early hip-hop parties.
Kurtis Blow was the first rapper to be signed by a major label (Mercury Records) in 1979.
Kurtis Blow's 'The Breaks' (1980) was the first certified gold rap single.
The Treacherous Three, featuring Kool Moe Dee, were among the first hip-hop groups to perform at a major venue (the Apollo Theater).
Eric B. & Rakim's 'Paid in Full' (1987) revolutionized lyrical complexity in hip-hop.
Rakim is often credited with introducing internal rhyme schemes and multisyllabic rhyming to rap.
Big Daddy Kane's intricate wordplay and rapid delivery influenced many later rappers including Jay-Z.
Slick Rick's 'Children's Story' (1988) is one of the most celebrated storytelling rap songs ever recorded.
The Juice Crew, based in Queensbridge, New York, included artists like MC Shan, Roxanne Shanté, and Big Daddy Kane.
The Bridge Wars (1986-1988) was a battle between the Juice Crew and Boogie Down Productions over hip-hop's origins.
KRS-One of Boogie Down Productions is often called 'The Teacha' for his socially conscious lyrics.
Ice-T is considered one of the pioneers of West Coast hip-hop and gangsta rap.
Ice-T's 'Colors' (1988) was a landmark West Coast hip-hop track tied to the Dennis Hopper film of the same name.
Too Short, from Oakland, California, was one of the first West Coast rappers to achieve nationwide success.
Digital Underground's 'The Humpty Dance' (1990) was a crossover hit that introduced Tupac Shakur as a backup dancer and roadie.
The Geto Boys, from Houston, Texas, helped put Southern hip-hop on the map in the late 1980s.
Scarface of the Geto Boys is often ranked among the greatest lyricists in hip-hop history.
DJ Screw from Houston invented the 'chopped and screwed' technique of slowing down and manipulating records.
UGK (Underground Kingz) from Port Arthur, Texas, were pivotal figures in Southern hip-hop.
Master P built No Limit Records into one of the most successful independent hip-hop labels of the 1990s.
Cash Money Records, founded by Bryan 'Birdman' Williams and Ronald 'Slim' Williams, became a dominant force in Southern hip-hop.
Three 6 Mafia won an Academy Award for Best Original Song for 'It's Hard out Here for a Pimp' from the film 'Hustle & Flow' in 2006.
Three 6 Mafia won their Oscar on March 5, 2006.
Nelly's 'Country Grammar' (2000) sold over 8.4 million copies in the United States, making it one of the best-selling hip-hop albums.
Ludacris, from Atlanta, earned both a Grammy and a SAG Award for his acting in 'Crash' (2004).
The Dirty South movement of the late 1990s and early 2000s brought national attention to hip-hop from the American South.
Lil Jon popularized crunk music, a high-energy subgenre of Southern hip-hop, in the early 2000s.
T.I. is often called the 'King of the South' and helped popularize trap music.
Trap music originated in Atlanta in the early 2000s, characterized by heavy bass, hi-hats, and lyrical themes about street life.
Young Jeezy's debut album 'Let's Get It: Thug Motivation 101' (2005) was a defining trap music release.
Gucci Mane is considered one of the key founders of trap music alongside T.I. and Young Jeezy.
Future helped pioneer the melodic trap subgenre with heavy use of Auto-Tune.
The Migos' 'Versace' (2013) introduced the triplet flow that became the dominant rap cadence of the 2010s.
Travis Scott's Astroworld Festival debuted in 2018 in Houston, Texas.
21 Savage was born in London, England, and moved to Atlanta at age 7.
Cardi B's 'Bodak Yellow' (2017) made her the first solo female rapper to top the Hot 100 since Lauryn Hill in 1998.
Cardi B was born Belcalis Marlenis Almánzar on October 11, 1992.
Nicki Minaj was the first female solo artist to have seven singles simultaneously on the Billboard Hot 100.
Nicki Minaj was born Onika Tanya Maraj on December 8, 1982, in Trinidad and Tobago.
Megan Thee Stallion coined the term 'Hot Girl Summer,' which became a cultural phenomenon in 2019.
Lil Nas X's 'Old Town Road' spent 19 weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100, breaking the record.
'Old Town Road' originally went viral on TikTok in 2019 before topping the charts.
Chance the Rapper's 'Coloring Book' (2016) was the first streaming-only album to win a Grammy.
Tyler, the Creator won the Grammy for Best Rap Album for 'Igor' in 2020.
Tyler, the Creator was born Tyler Gregory Okonma on March 6, 1991.
Odd Future (OFWGKTA) was a Los Angeles-based hip-hop collective that launched the careers of Tyler, the Creator, Frank Ocean, and Earl Sweatshirt.
J. Cole went platinum with his album '2014 Forest Hills Drive' (2014) with no featured artists, a rare achievement.
J. Cole was born Jermaine Lamarr Cole on January 28, 1985, in Frankfurt, Germany.
Childish Gambino's 'This Is America' (2018) debuted at number one on the Hot 100 and won four Grammy Awards.
Post Malone's 'Rockstar' (2017) spent eight weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100.
The SoundCloud rap movement of the mid-2010s launched artists like Lil Uzi Vert, XXXTentacion, and Juice WRLD.
Juice WRLD's freestyle 'Fire in the Booth' showcased his ability to freestyle for over an hour without stopping.
Pop Smoke helped popularize Brooklyn drill music before his death in February 2020.
UK drill music influenced the development of Brooklyn drill in the late 2010s.
Stormzy became the first grime artist to headline Glastonbury Festival in 2019.
Grime music originated in London in the early 2000s, blending elements of UK garage, jungle, and hip-hop.
Skepta's 'Konnichiwa' (2016) won the Mercury Prize, a prestigious UK music award.
The Pharcyde's 'Bizarre Ride II the Pharcyde' (1992) is a classic of alternative hip-hop from Los Angeles.
MF DOOM was known for his signature metal mask and complex rhyme schemes.
MF DOOM and Madlib's collaboration album 'Madvillainy' (2004) is considered a masterpiece of underground hip-hop.
Aesop Rock has been calculated to have the largest vocabulary of any hip-hop artist, surpassing Shakespeare in unique word count.
Atmosphere, from Minneapolis, helped build the Rhymesayers Entertainment independent hip-hop label.
Tech N9ne founded Strange Music, one of the largest independent hip-hop labels in the United States.
Immortal Technique is known for his politically charged lyrics and for independently releasing his albums.
The Roots are known as one of the few hip-hop groups to use a full live band.
The Roots have served as the house band on 'The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon' since 2014.
Common (formerly Common Sense) is celebrated for his conscious hip-hop and later won an Oscar for the song 'Glory' from the film 'Selma.'
Mos Def and Talib Kweli formed Black Star, whose 1998 self-titled album is a landmark of conscious hip-hop.
Talib Kweli's name means 'the seeker of truth' in Arabic.
Pharrell Williams and Chad Hugo formed The Neptunes, one of the most successful production duos in hip-hop history.
Timbaland's innovative production style, incorporating global sounds and syncopated beats, defined the late-1990s and early-2000s hip-hop sound.
Swizz Beatz produced hit records for DMX, Jay-Z, and many others, becoming one of hip-hop's most prolific beatmakers.
Just Blaze is known for his soulful production style and work on Jay-Z's 'The Blueprint' (2001).
DJ Premier of Gang Starr is considered one of the greatest hip-hop producers of all time.
Pete Rock is renowned for his use of jazz samples and the SP-1200 sampler in hip-hop production.
The Akai MPC (Music Production Center) series has been a staple of hip-hop production since the late 1980s.
J Dilla's 'Donuts' (2006) was released on February 7, 2006, just three days before his death.
J Dilla is considered one of the most influential producers in hip-hop, known for his off-kilter timing and soulful beats.
Madlib is known for producing beats from rare vinyl samples sourced from record stores around the world.
RZA of Wu-Tang Clan produced the group's first several albums and developed a gritty, sample-heavy production style.
Metro Boomin became one of the most sought-after producers of the 2010s, working with Future, 21 Savage, and Migos.
Mike Dean has been a key producer and collaborator for Kanye West, Travis Scott, and many Houston artists.
The SP-1200 sampler, with its lo-fi 12-bit sound, is considered a classic hip-hop production tool.
Auto-Tune was first notably used in hip-hop by T-Pain, who popularized the vocal effect in the mid-2000s.
Kanye West's '808s & Heartbreak' (2008) heavily influenced the use of Auto-Tune and emotional themes in modern rap.
'808s & Heartbreak' was released on November 24, 2008.
Lil Wayne is often credited with popularizing mixtape culture in the 2000s with his 'Dedication' and 'Da Drought' series.
50 Cent's 'Get Rich or Die Tryin'' (2003) sold 872,000 copies in its first four days of release.
50 Cent was born Curtis James Jackson III on July 6, 1975, in Queens, New York.
Eminem's 'The Marshall Mathers LP' (2000) sold 1.76 million copies in its first week, a record at the time.
The 8 Mile film (2002), loosely based on Eminem's life, grossed over $240 million worldwide.
Eminem's 'Lose Yourself' from the '8 Mile' soundtrack won the Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Dr. Dre discovered and mentored artists including Snoop Dogg, Eminem, 50 Cent, and Kendrick Lamar.
Aftermath Entertainment, founded by Dr. Dre in 1996, became one of the most successful hip-hop labels.
Dr. Dre sold Beats Electronics to Apple for approximately $3 billion in 2014, one of the largest deals in music history.
Jay-Z co-founded Roc-A-Fella Records with Damon Dash and Kareem 'Biggs' Burke in 1995.
Jay-Z became the first hip-hop artist to become a billionaire, according to Forbes.
Roc Nation, Jay-Z's entertainment company founded in 2008, manages athletes and musicians and operates a record label.
Diddy (Sean Combs) founded Bad Boy Records in 1993, launching the careers of The Notorious B.I.G., Mase, and others.
Death Row Records, co-founded by Suge Knight and Dr. Dre in 1991, was home to Tupac, Snoop Dogg, and others.
Ruthless Records, founded by Eazy-E and Jerry Heller, was N.W.A's original label.
Top Dawg Entertainment (TDE) is the label behind Kendrick Lamar, ScHoolboy Q, SZA, and Ab-Soul.
Quality Control Music, founded in Atlanta, launched the careers of Migos, Lil Yachty, and Lil Baby.
Dreamville Records, founded by J. Cole, is both a record label and an annual music festival.
The first rap Grammy Award was given in 1989 to DJ Jazzy Jeff & The Fresh Prince for 'Parents Just Don't Understand.'
Many artists boycotted the first rap Grammy ceremony because the category was not televised.
The Grammy for Best Rap Album was first awarded in 1996 to Naughty by Nature for 'Poverty's Paradise.'
Cypress Hill was the first Latino American hip-hop group to have a platinum album.
The Fugees' 'The Score' (1996) sold over 22 million copies worldwide.
Wyclef Jean of the Fugees went on to have a successful solo career and even ran for president of Haiti in 2010.
Bone Thugs-n-Harmony from Cleveland blended rapid-fire rapping with melodic harmonies.
Bone Thugs-n-Harmony's 'Tha Crossroads' won a Grammy for Best Rap Performance by a Duo or Group in 1997.
Busta Rhymes is known for his rapid-fire delivery and energetic music videos.
DMX was the first artist to have his first five studio albums debut at number one on the Billboard 200.
DMX was born Earl Simmons on December 18, 1970, in Mount Vernon, New York.
Method Man of Wu-Tang Clan was the first member to release a solo album, 'Tical' (1994).
Ol' Dirty Bastard of Wu-Tang Clan was known for his eccentric personality and raw vocal style.
GZA's 'Liquid Swords' (1995) is considered one of the best solo Wu-Tang albums.
Redman and Method Man's chemistry led to a collaborative album, 'Blackout!' (1999), and a sitcom.
Xzibit became widely known for hosting the MTV show 'Pimp My Ride' (2004-2007).
The Diplomats (Dipset) from Harlem, led by Cam'ron, influenced hip-hop fashion with their pink fur coats and colorful style.
Cam'ron's pink fur coat and matching Range Rover became iconic symbols of early-2000s hip-hop fashion.
Hip-hop fashion has evolved from Adidas tracksuits and gold chains in the 1980s to designer brands and streetwear today.
FUBU (For Us, By Us), founded by Daymond John, was one of the first major hip-hop fashion brands.
Pharrell Williams co-founded the streetwear brand Billionaire Boys Club and the sneaker line Ice Cream.
Kanye West's Yeezy brand with Adidas became one of the most successful celebrity fashion collaborations in history.
Run-D.M.C.'s endorsement deal with Adidas in 1986 was the first sneaker deal in hip-hop history.
Grillz (decorative dental jewelry) became a mainstream hip-hop fashion accessory in the 2000s, popularized by Southern rappers.
Dapper Dan from Harlem became famous for creating custom luxury hip-hop fashion using logos from brands like Gucci and Louis Vuitton in the 1980s.
Sean John, Diddy's fashion line launched in 1998, won the CFDA Menswear Designer of the Year award in 2004.
The boom bap sound, characterized by hard-hitting drums and jazzy samples, defined East Coast hip-hop in the 1990s.
Cloud rap, characterized by ethereal production and hazy atmospheres, emerged in the early 2010s with artists like Lil B and A$AP Rocky.
Drill music originated in Chicago around 2010-2012 with artists like Chief Keef, Lil Durk, and King Louie.
Chief Keef's 'Love Sosa' (2012) was a viral hit that helped bring Chicago drill to national prominence.
Chance the Rapper was born on April 16, 1993, in Chicago, Illinois.
Kanye West was born on June 8, 1977, in Atlanta, Georgia, and raised in Chicago.
Twista, from Chicago, held the Guinness World Record for fastest rapper at 598 syllables in 55 seconds.
The chopped and screwed style originated in Houston and involves slowing down the tempo and applying effects to music.
Pimp C of UGK was a central figure in Houston hip-hop culture until his death in 2007.
Outkast's André 3000 is widely regarded as one of the most versatile and creative MCs in hip-hop history.
Outkast's 'Hey Ya!' (2003) spent nine weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100.
Goodie Mob, from Atlanta, was one of the first groups in the Dungeon Family collective alongside Outkast.
CeeLo Green, later famous for 'Crazy' with Gnarls Barkley, was originally a member of Goodie Mob.
Killer Mike from Atlanta has been active in hip-hop since the early 2000s and later formed Run the Jewels with El-P.
Run the Jewels released their debut album for free on the internet in 2013.
El-P, originally from Company Flow, is a respected underground rapper and producer from Brooklyn.
Freestyle rapping, or improvised rapping, is a core skill in hip-hop culture.
The freestyle battle scene has been a testing ground for rappers since the earliest days of hip-hop.
BET's '106 & Park' freestyle Fridays (2000s) helped launch careers and showcase battle rap skills.
Scribble Jam, held annually in Cincinnati from 1996 to 2008, was one of the largest hip-hop festivals featuring battle rap.
The Ultimate Rap League (URL) is one of the premier battle rap leagues in the world.
King of the Dot (KOTD) is a prominent battle rap league based in Canada.
Eminem's battle rap skills were showcased in the 2002 film '8 Mile,' based loosely on his experiences in Detroit.
Loaded Lux vs. Calicoe (2012) is often cited as one of the greatest rap battles of all time.
The Spit Your Game documentary series helped popularize battle rap on YouTube.
Hip-hop lyrics have been used as evidence in criminal trials, sparking debates about artistic expression and legal rights.
Several U.S. states have introduced 'Rap Music on Trial' bills to limit the use of song lyrics as courtroom evidence.
Kendrick Lamar's 'good kid, m.A.A.d city' (2012) is often described as a 'short film' for its narrative structure.
Kendrick Lamar's 'To Pimp a Butterfly' (2015) blended hip-hop with jazz, funk, and spoken word to critical acclaim.
Anderson .Paak blends hip-hop with R&B, soul, and funk, and is also a skilled drummer.
Mac Miller's posthumous album 'Circles' (2020) was completed by producer Jon Brion after Miller's death.
Mac Miller was born Malcolm James McCormick on January 19, 1992, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
Nipsey Hussle was a community activist and rapper from Crenshaw, Los Angeles, who was tragically killed on March 31, 2019.
Nipsey Hussle's 'Victory Lap' (2018) was nominated for the Grammy for Best Rap Album.
A$AP Rocky co-founded the A$AP Mob collective in Harlem, blending fashion, music, and visual art.
A$AP Rocky's debut mixtape 'Live.Love.A$AP' (2011) was a critical hit for its psychedelic, cloud-rap influenced sound.
Pusha T served as the president of Kanye West's G.O.O.D. Music label.
Pusha T and his brother Malice formed Clipse, whose 'Lord Willin'' (2002) is a cult classic.
Pharoahe Monch is considered one of the most technically skilled rappers of all time.
Black Thought of The Roots is frequently cited as one of the greatest live performers in hip-hop.
Snoop Dogg's real name is Calvin Cordozar Broadus Jr., and his nickname came from his mother.
Snoop Dogg briefly renamed himself Snoop Lion in 2012 to release a reggae album.
Ice Cube left N.W.A over financial disputes and released the classic solo album 'AmeriKKKa's Most Wanted' (1990).
Ice Cube went on to become a successful actor, starring in the 'Friday' and 'Barbershop' film franchises.
Eazy-E, born Eric Lynn Wright, was a founding member of N.W.A and Ruthless Records.
Eazy-E passed away on March 26, 1995, from complications of AIDS.
MC Hammer's 'U Can't Touch This' (1990) sampled Rick James' 'Super Freak' and became one of the biggest rap hits of the era.
Vanilla Ice's 'Ice Ice Baby' (1990) was the first hip-hop single to top the Billboard Hot 100.
The Beastie Boys were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame on April 14, 2012.
The Beastie Boys originally started as a hardcore punk band before transitioning to hip-hop.
Will Smith, as The Fresh Prince, won the first Grammy Award ever given for a hip-hop performance.
DJ Jazzy Jeff (Jeff Townes) is considered one of the greatest turntablists in hip-hop history.
LL Cool J was one of the first rappers to achieve crossover success with ballads like 'I Need Love' (1987).
LL Cool J was born James Todd Smith on January 14, 1968, in Bay Shore, New York.
Heavy D, born Dwight Arif Myers, was known as 'The Overweight Lover' and helped popularize New Jack Swing.
New Jack Swing, a fusion of hip-hop and R&B, was pioneered by producer Teddy Riley in the late 1980s.
Arrested Development won the Grammy for Best New Artist in 1993, one of few hip-hop acts to receive that honor.
Coolio's 'Gangsta's Paradise' (1995) was the number-one single of the year and won a Grammy.
The Notorious B.I.G.'s 'Life After Death' was released on March 25, 1997, just 16 days after his murder.
Tupac recorded an enormous volume of music; dozens of posthumous albums have been released since his death.
Tupac acted in films like 'Juice' (1992), 'Poetic Justice' (1993), and 'Above the Rim' (1994).
Suge Knight co-founded Death Row Records and was a controversial figure in 1990s hip-hop.
Lil Kim's debut album 'Hard Core' (1996) pushed boundaries for female rappers with its explicit content.
Foxy Brown released her debut album 'Ill Na Na' in 1996 at just 17 years old.
Eve was the first female rapper on Ruff Ryders Entertainment and later starred in her own TV sitcom.
Da Brat was the first solo female rapper to have a platinum-selling album with 'Funkdafied' (1994).
Roxanne Shanté recorded 'Roxanne's Revenge' in 1984 at only 14 years old.
The Roxanne Wars of 1984-1985 produced over 100 answer records in response to UTFO's 'Roxanne, Roxanne.'
Jean Grae, born in Cape Town, South Africa, is regarded as one of the most skilled female MCs in underground hip-hop.
Rapsody's 'Laila's Wisdom' (2017) was nominated for the Grammy for Best Rap Album.
Little Simz, from London, won the Mercury Prize for her album 'Sometimes I Might Be Introvert' (2022).
Tierra Whack's 'Whack World' (2018) consisted of 15 tracks, each exactly one minute long, with accompanying music videos.
Doja Cat, born Amala Ratna Zandile Dlamini, blends rap with pop, R&B, and internet culture.
GloRilla, from Memphis, signed to Yo Gotti's CMG label and broke through in 2022 with 'F.N.F.'
Ice Spice, from the Bronx, became one of the fastest-rising rap stars of 2023.
The term 'diss track' refers to a song made specifically to insult or challenge another artist.
Tupac's 'Hit 'Em Up' (1996) targeting The Notorious B.I.G. is one of the most famous diss tracks ever.
Jay-Z and Nas's rivalry produced legendary diss tracks including 'Ether' and 'Takeover.'
Drake and Pusha T's 2018 feud included Pusha T's 'The Story of Adidon,' which revealed personal information about Drake.
The Kendrick Lamar and Drake feud of 2024 produced multiple diss tracks and dominated social media.
Kendrick Lamar's 'Not Like Us' (2024) became one of the most viral diss tracks in hip-hop history.
50 Cent and Ja Rule's feud in the early 2000s significantly impacted both artists' careers.
The Bridge Is Over' by Boogie Down Productions (1987) is one of hip-hop's earliest and most famous diss tracks.
LL Cool J and Kool Moe Dee had one of the first major hip-hop beefs in the late 1980s.
Canibus and LL Cool J's feud in the late 1990s produced several diss records.
The concept album has become a significant format in hip-hop, with artists creating thematic narratives across entire records.
Kendrick Lamar's 'good kid, m.A.A.d city' is structured as a coming-of-age story set in Compton.
Kanye West's 'My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy' (2010) is often cited as one of the greatest albums of the 21st century.
Kanye West's 'My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy' was released on November 22, 2010.
Tyler, the Creator's 'Flower Boy' (2017) marked a creative evolution with more introspective themes.
Vince Staples' 'Big Fish Theory' (2017) incorporated electronic and dance music into hip-hop.
Danny Brown's 'Atrocity Exhibition' (2016) was named after a Joy Division song and blended experimental production with rap.
Earl Sweatshirt's 'Some Rap Songs' (2018) pushed the boundaries of lo-fi, abstract hip-hop.
JPEGMAFIA (Peggy) is known for his experimental approach, blending noise, glitch, and industrial music with rap.
Denzel Curry from Carol City, Florida, gained fame with his aggressive style and concept albums.
Playboi Carti's 'Whole Lotta Red' (2020) divided fans but has grown a cult following for its punk-influenced sound.
Young Thug's unconventional vocal style and fashion choices have influenced a generation of rappers.
Young Thug was indicted on RICO charges in May 2022 as part of a wide-ranging criminal case in Atlanta.
Lil Baby's 'My Turn' (2020) was the best-selling album of 2020 in the United States.
DaBaby's 'Rockstar' featuring Roddy Ricch (2020) spent seven weeks at number one on the Hot 100.
Roddy Ricch's 'The Box' (2020) topped the Hot 100 for 11 weeks.
Jack Harlow, from Louisville, Kentucky, broke out in 2020 with 'Whats Poppin.'
NBA YoungBoy has one of the highest YouTube subscriber counts among hip-hop artists.
Polo G, from Chicago, broke through with 'Pop Out' (2019) featuring Lil Tjay.
The rise of streaming platforms like Spotify and Apple Music has fundamentally changed how hip-hop is distributed and consumed.
Hip-hop became the most consumed music genre in the United States in 2017, surpassing rock.
Drake's 'Scorpion' (2018) was the first album to surpass one billion streams on Spotify in its first week.
Bad Bunny, who blends reggaeton with hip-hop, was the most-streamed artist on Spotify from 2020 to 2023.
The hip-hop genre has expanded globally, with thriving scenes in France, South Korea, Japan, Nigeria, and Brazil.
BTS collaborated with various American hip-hop artists, helping bridge K-pop and hip-hop.
French hip-hop is the second-largest hip-hop market in the world after the United States.
MC Solaar was one of the first French-language rappers to achieve international recognition in the 1990s.
Nigerian hip-hop, sometimes called 'Afrobeats rap,' has produced artists like Olamide and M.I Abaga.
Japanese hip-hop emerged in the 1980s and has since developed its own distinct culture and sound.
Keith Shocklee, Hank Shocklee, and Eric Sadler formed the Bomb Squad, Public Enemy's legendary production team.
Public Enemy's Chuck D once described rap as 'the CNN of the ghetto.'
The sample clearance process can be extremely expensive, with some samples costing hundreds of thousands of dollars.
Biz Markie's 'Alone Again' (1991) led to a landmark sampling lawsuit that changed how samples are legally cleared in hip-hop.
The 1991 Grand Upright Music, Ltd. v. Warner Bros. Records case established that uncleared sampling constitutes copyright infringement.
Many classic hip-hop songs sample James Brown, the most sampled artist in the genre's history.
'Funky Drummer' by James Brown is the most sampled breakbeat in music history.
The Amen break, a six-second drum solo from The Winstons' 'Amen, Brother' (1969), is one of the most used samples in hip-hop and electronic music.
Kanye West is known for his innovative use of soul music samples, often speeding up and pitch-shifting classic recordings.
FL Studio (formerly FruityLoops) has become one of the most popular digital audio workstations for hip-hop producers.
Soulja Boy's 'Crank That' (2007) was one of the first hip-hop hits to achieve viral success through the internet.
Soulja Boy is often credited as one of the first artists to use social media and the internet to launch a mainstream music career.
WorldStarHipHop, founded in 2005, became one of the most popular platforms for hip-hop music videos and viral content.
DatPiff was a major platform for free mixtape distribution in the 2000s and 2010s.
Lil B 'The BasedGod' was a pioneer of internet-era hip-hop, releasing hundreds of free tracks online.
Drake originally gained fame as an actor on the Canadian TV show 'Degrassi: The Next Generation.'
OVO Sound, Drake's record label, was co-founded with Noah '40' Shebib and Oliver El-Khatib.
40 (Noah Shebib) is Drake's primary producer and is known for his ambient, atmospheric beats.
Logic's album 'Everybody' (2017) debuted at number one and explored themes of race, anxiety, and belonging.
Logic's song '1-800-273-8255' (2017), named after the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline, was credited with increasing calls to the hotline.
Joyner Lucas gained viral fame with his visual album 'I'm Not Racist' (2017).
Machine Gun Kelly transitioned from rap to pop-punk rock in 2020 with 'Tickets to My Downfall.'
YG, from Compton, is known for his West Coast hip-hop style and the hit 'My Hitta' (2014).
Nipsey Hussle released his mixtape 'Crenshaw' in 2013, selling physical copies for $100 each.
Wiz Khalifa's 'See You Again' (2015) from 'Furious 7' became one of the most-viewed videos on YouTube.
Wiz Khalifa was born Cameron Jibril Thomaz on September 8, 1987, in Minot, North Dakota.
Kid Cudi's 'Man on the Moon: The End of Day' (2009) pioneered a more emotional and introspective style of rap.
Kid Cudi's influence can be heard in the music of Travis Scott, Kanye West's later work, and many emo-rap artists.
ScHoolboy Q's 'Oxymoron' (2014) debuted at number one on the Billboard 200.
Isaiah Rashad, from Chattanooga, Tennessee, is a member of TDE known for his introspective Southern style.
Joey Bada$$, from Brooklyn, co-founded the Pro Era hip-hop collective.
Flatbush Zombies, also from Brooklyn, are known for their psychedelic and horrorcore-influenced hip-hop.
The Underachievers, from Flatbush, blend spiritual themes and third-eye philosophy with hip-hop.
Griselda Records, led by Westside Gunn, Benny the Butcher, and Conway the Machine, revived gritty East Coast boom-bap in the late 2010s.
Westside Gunn's 'Pray for Paris' (2020) sampled rare soul and jazz records while featuring high-fashion themes.
Freddie Gibbs, from Gary, Indiana, has collaborated with Madlib on critically acclaimed albums like 'Piñata' (2014) and 'Bandana' (2019).
Russ achieved success by independently recording, producing, and releasing his own music before signing a major deal.
Noname, from Chicago, is known for her poetic, spoken-word-influenced rap style.
Saba, also from Chicago, created the Pivot Gang collective and released the acclaimed album 'Care for Me' (2018).
JID, from Atlanta, is signed to Dreamville and known for his rapid-fire flow and lyrical dexterity.
Cordae's debut album 'The Lost Boy' (2019) was nominated for the Grammy for Best Rap Album.
Rico Nasty blends hip-hop with punk, creating a style she calls 'sugar trap.'
City Girls, from Miami, are known for their bold party anthems and ties to Quality Control Music.
Saweetie's 'My Type' (2019) sampled Petey Pablo's 'Freek-a-Leek' and became a viral hit.
Latto, originally known as Miss Mulatto, won the first season of Lifetime's 'The Rap Game' at age 16.
The 'mumble rap' label emerged in the mid-2010s to describe a style favoring melody and ad-libs over lyrical complexity.
The debate between lyrical rap and melodic/trap rap has been ongoing since the mid-2010s.
Ghostwriting has been a controversial topic in hip-hop, with many fans and artists valuing authenticity in writing one's own lyrics.
Dr. Dre is widely known to use ghostwriters, but his production skills and ear for talent are unquestioned.
Nas and Jay-Z's rivalry in the early 2000s is considered one of the greatest lyrical battles in hip-hop history.
Jay-Z's 'The Blueprint' was released on September 11, 2001, the same day as the terrorist attacks.
Kanye West produced several tracks on Jay-Z's 'The Blueprint,' including 'Izzo (H.O.V.A.).'
Watch the Throne (2011), the collaborative album by Jay-Z and Kanye West, debuted at number one worldwide.
Kids See Ghosts (2018) was a collaborative album between Kanye West and Kid Cudi.
The term 'bars' in hip-hop refers to lines of lyrics, with skilled rappers described as having 'bars.'
A 'cipher' is an informal gathering of rappers freestyling together, often in a circle.
The term 'flow' refers to the rhythmic pattern and cadence a rapper uses when delivering their verses.
Multisyllabic rhyming, pioneered by Rakim, involves rhyming multiple syllables within or across lines.
Doubletime rapping involves delivering lyrics at twice the normal speed while maintaining clarity.
Chopper-style rapping, popular in the Midwest, involves extremely fast speeds and was perfected by artists like Tech N9ne and Twista.
Ghostface Killah of Wu-Tang Clan is known for his vivid storytelling and stream-of-consciousness lyrical style.
Inspectah Deck of Wu-Tang Clan delivered one of hip-hop's most celebrated verses on 'Triumph' (1997).
Mobb Deep's 'The Infamous' (1995) is considered one of the definitive albums of the hardcore East Coast rap movement.
Prodigy of Mobb Deep passed away on June 20, 2017, at the age of 42.
AZ's 'Doe or Die' (1995) is a respected but often overlooked East Coast classic.
Capone-N-Noreaga (C-N-N) from Queens are known for the street anthem 'T.O.N.Y.' (1997).
Fat Joe, from the Bronx, has been active since 1993 and scored a massive hit with 'Lean Back' (2004) with Terror Squad.
Big Pun was the first solo Latino rapper to have a platinum-selling album with 'Capital Punishment' (1998).
Big Pun passed away on February 7, 2000, at the age of 28.
Jadakiss, Styles P, and Sheek Louch form The LOX, originally signed to Bad Boy Records.
Fabolous, from Brooklyn, has been a consistent presence in hip-hop since his debut in 2001.
Lupe Fiasco's 'Food & Liquor' (2006) was a critically acclaimed debut blending skateboarding culture with conscious rap.
Talib Kweli has released over ten studio albums and is known for his independent approach to the music industry.
Brother Ali, signed to Rhymesayers, is an albino rapper known for his powerful voice and socially conscious lyrics.
Sage Francis is a pioneer of the spoken-word and indie hip-hop scene.
